dialogue.utterance: Hi, I'm Tyler, an EMT. What made you call 911 today?
I'm having heavy vaginal bleeding and abdominal cramps since this morning. I feel very weak.
How long have you been experiencing this?
You mentioned cramps and bleeding. Is there anything else you want to share before we dig deeper into your symptoms?
Let me check your alertness. Can you tell me your name, the date, and where you are right now?
My name is Rachel. Today is December 2nd, and I'm in my car in [CITY].
Great, and do you know why you called 911 today?
Because I felt too weak to drive myself to the hospital.
Your GCS is 15 - you're fully alert and oriented. I don't see confusion or any issues with your mental status.
Your airway is clear and patent. You don't appear to have any signs of obstruction.
I can see your chest rising and falling equally. Your breathing rate is 16 breaths per minute, which is normal.
Your eyes are responding well; they track and follow me, and you seem to be attentive.
Your pulse is strong, but your blood pressure is lower than average at 92 over 63. That's consistent with your fast heart rate of 100.
Let me confirm that. Your pulse is 100, and it's relatively regular.
Continuing on, you're breathing at 16 per minute and evenly. That's comfortable and within normal limits.
Blood pressure taken is 92 over 63. That does show some mild hypotension.
We're running a quick read on your oxygen levels. You're at 100% on room air, so that's reassuring.
I'm placing the EKG leads now. Your heart rhythm is showing sinus tachycardia initially, which matches your rapid pulse.
Your skin is pale and a bit ashen, but it's dry and warm. I don't see any edema or swelling. There is ongoing vaginal bleeding.
How would you rate your pain right now on a scale from 1 to 10?
A six.
When did the pain start? Has it changed in intensity or location throughout the day?
It began this morning and got worse around noon.
Can you describe the cramping or the bleeding a bit more? Did it feel sharp, throbbing?
The pain is more of a constant pressure, not sharp. It comes and goes sometimes.
This is your fifth pregnancy, right? How far along are you?
Yes, I'm about eight weeks along. No previous issues or complications.
Are you currently taking any medications or have any known allergies?
No medications and no allergies.
When the symptoms worsened, what did you do or feel like doing next?
I tried to drive to the hospital, but I felt so weak and dizzy I had to pull over and call 911.
Did you feel drowsy or lightheaded at all before or during your trip?
Yes, very dizzy, and I didn't feel I was going to make it. I was real tired and scared.
Our skin exam doesn't show any rashes, bruises, or scars. I don't see any skin turgor changes or swelling.
I'll quickly check your head and neck. Your pupils are reactive, and I don't see any signs of strain or stiffness. You mentioned dizziness - is there a point it gets worse?
Looking at your ears, nose, and throat, I don't see any drainage or obstructions.
Taking a listen to your lungs, they are clear with no wheezing or crackles.
I'm gently palpating your abdomen. You have some cramping and tenderness but no masses or distension.
Persistent vaginal bleeding is noted. No signs of foul discharge or clots are being reported at the moment.
Your extremities look unremarkable. No swelling or deformity observed.
Your pulse has dropped to 91 now but is still tachycardic.
Blood pressure continues to hover at 89 over 62, still slightly low.
Your oxygen level is at 98%, but still within safe parameters.
Your rhythm is now normal sinus, per the EKG we just ran.
We've remeasured and saw a 100% reading next - great improvement.
We're applying 4-lead ECG monitoring for continuous heart rhythm assessment.
We're also setting up an 18-gauge IV line in your left arm for normal saline to help with circulation.
IV started successfully with one attempt. I've already begun giving warm fluid solution.
Earlier, the EKG read tachycardia, but we've confirmed now it's in normal rhythm.
Your heart is now working at 98 beats per minute.
The blood pressure measurement is now 86 over 65.
You're stable for now, and the IV is secure.
We're preparing to move you into the ambulance. You'll be safe for the trip.
I'm feeling lightheaded, but the cramping hasn't gotten worse.
That lightheaded feeling and fatigue are common after blood loss. We'll keep an eye on your vitals.
We've radioed [COMPANY_NAME] - they're expecting you and will be waiting.
Your pulse has improved to 88 now.
Blood pressure is 90 over 64 - that's a good trend.
Oxygen is at 100% now, excellent. We've already given you 300 mL of fluids. That should help support better circulation.
The IV continues without any issues. We are stopping the flow once your systolic reaches 90.
Out now, EKG continues in normal sinus rhythm.
We've loaded you and are beginning the cold transport. Vital signs and IV are under control.
You're doing well for now. We'll communicate regularly.
We'll give an IV report as soon as you're settled in the hospital.
Other procedures will follow as you get clinical level care from ER staff.
Final pulse reading just before arrival is 98.
Blood pressure is at 93 over 63 now.
Oxygen saturation stays at 100% on board.
Final EKG continues confirming a stable sinus rhythm.
Ambulance is now at ER entrance. Handover is imminent.
You're on track for delivery to the emergency department at [CITY]. They've been informed about all your vitals, EKG, and bleeding.
You look a bit tired now, but you're stable. We'll keep you supported on the way in.
You'll see OB/GYN and imaging next, and the ER is prepared for your arrival.
We stopped IV flow at 300mL when systolic reached 90. IV is ready for ER to restart if needed.
The IV site is clean and undisturbed. ER will pick up from there.
The patient shows signs consistent with protocol OB/GYN - pre-term labor (protocol 6-12) and Medical - Hypotension/Shock (Nontrauma) (protocol 3-13).
Initial vitals were 92/63, heart rate of 100, and sinus tachycardia.
300 mL of normal saline have improved blood pressure and oxygenation.
IV was stopped at 90 systolic, and ongoing vitals are stable with A&Ox4 status.
Informed consent was signed, and all treatments were provided with full documentation on file.
Patient will be delivered directly to the emergency department as per protocol.
Vital signs and EKG were logged every few minutes en route and showed no major deterioration.
Final temperature was 97.1, with no signs of fever or chills.
Blood glucose was not obtained due to no relevant oral intake since symptoms began.
No medications were administered during transport. Full care and handoff are in place.
All vitals remained within monitored guidelines throughout the transfer, including 4-lead EKG monitoring.
Continuous communication with the patient and rapid response time led to a safe and stable transfer.
EMS complied with [CITY] and standard protocol requirements.
No complications occurred during transport, and EKG, IV, and vitals were maintained.
Ongoing condition remains consistent. IV and fluid assessment at the time of arrival were reported.
She expressed feeling drowsy and dizzy since the onset of symptoms, which is aligned with her condition.
At one point her oxygen read 99% en route - now back up to 100%.
Transfer fulfilled per hospital and EMS policies with full transparency and professional care.
The patient was kept alert, oriented, and in good condition for the ER to continue her care.
We did not encounter any issues of safety or technical challenges throughout transport.
The EMS teams communicated effectively with dispatch, ER, and the patient. Supported the transfer without need for an emergency code.
Vital monitoring and patient updates were consistent with best practices in managing this type of condition.
The transfer to the ER is complete. Thanks again for your trust, Rachel.
Support team will stand by for your recovery update.
EMS teams are returning to service following a safe and successful patient transfer.
